doc.title: Equity method — 20% to 50% influence
doc.event: Pell Co. buys 30% of Quin Inc. for $300,000, giving it significant influence. Quin then reports $100,000 of net income and pays $20,000 of total dividends. Prepare all entries and compute the investment's year-end carrying value.

variants.label: Buy 30% · accrue share of income · dividends shrink the stake

lines.label: at cost
lines.why: In at cost — then the account starts breathing with the investee.
lines.trap: 20–50% is a PRESUMPTION of influence, not a law — board seats, tech dependency, or contracts can push a 15% stake into the equity method (or block a 30% one out).

lines.label: price paid
lines.trap: No fair-value marks from here on — influence stakes don't remeasure to market.

lines.why: Your share of their earnings grows YOUR asset — you influence the machine that earned it.
lines.trap: Accrues when THEY report income, not when cash moves — the equity method is accrual accounting applied to ownership.

lines.label: your 30% slice
lines.why: Income on your books in the same period they earn it.
lines.trap: Investee reports a LOSS? Same entry, flipped: debit Investment Loss, credit the investment.

lines.why: Your share of the distribution arrives.
lines.trap: THE equity-method trap: this is NOT revenue.

lines.label: dividends REDUCE the stake
lines.why: You already counted this money when they EARNED it (entry above). The dividend just converts part of your investment into cash — a return OF capital, not income.
lines.trap: Crediting Dividend Revenue here double-counts the earnings. If one line separates fair-value from equity-method on the exam, it's this one.

computationPaths.narration: The investment account as a running story: cost + share of income − share of dividends. That balance is also the answer to 'what would the gain/loss be if sold' questions.

memorize.body: Equity-method carrying value = cost + your % of investee income − your % of investee dividends. Income when they EARN; dividends shrink the INVESTMENT.
Fair value method: dividends = revenue. Equity method: dividends = refund. Ask 'did I already count these earnings?' — under influence, yes you did.
If purchase price exceeds your share of the investee's BOOK value, the excess tied to depreciable assets amortizes against Investment Income each year — the P-level twist on this machinery.

questions.prompt: 30% stake bought for 300,000. Investee then reports 100,000 income and pays 20,000 total dividends. Year-end carrying value of the investment =

distractors.feedback: 330,000 adds the income share but treats the 6,000 dividend as revenue instead of reducing the investment. Equity method: 300,000 + 30,000 − 6,000 = 324,000.
Frozen-at-cost is the OLD cost method. Under the equity method the account moves with the investee: +30,000 income share, −6,000 dividends → 324,000.

accountBank.account: Unrealized Holding Gain or Loss — Income

build.scaffold: Three beats: cost in; their income grows your asset (and your income); their dividends shrink your asset (never revenue). All three decoys are fair-value-method furniture — wrong method, wrong room.
